segments.s339-g65: از این کار is a prepositional phrase ('from / because of this affair') marking the cause or starting point. خسرو here names the person whose presence is left (the king); Persian often places the person/thing affected before the verb. به بیرون شدیم is a past-tense verbal phrase with شدیم (past plural of شدن) used intransitively to mean 'we went out / departed', with به بیرون as its locative complement.
segments.s339-g66: سوی کار لهراسب is a directional phrase: سوی 'toward' + the genitive noun phrase کار لهراسب ('Lohrasp's affairs'). بازآمدیم is past-plural 'we returned / came back' and is the main verb of this clause, linking the action to the preceding prepositional phrase.
segments.s339-g67: به پیروزی is a prepositional/optative phrase ('to / for victory') expressing the wished-for outcome; شهریار بلند is the head noun plus its adjective ('the lofty / exalted king'), with the adjective following the noun as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s339-g68: کز introduces a relative/causal clause meaning 'from/for whom' or 'for which'. اویست here is a fused poetic form (او + است) functioning as 'it is in him / it is from him' (an archaic contraction common in classical verse). امید نیک و گزند is the clause's content: 'hope (of) good and harm' — i.e., both good (nīk) and harm (gazand) are associated with him. Note: اویست in the lexicon entry given is a proper name sense which does not apply here.
segments.s339-g69: به نیکی is an adverbial phrase ('with goodness / to goodness') modifying the verb. رساند is past-third-person 'he brought / caused to reach', and دل دوستان is the object phrase meaning 'the hearts of the friends' (noun + modifier), so the clause means 'he brought goodness to the friends' hearts.'
segments.s339-g70: گزند آید is an impersonal/presentive clause 'harm comes / will come'. از وی marks source 'from him'. به ناراستان gives the target/recipient with به + adjective-noun 'ناراستان' meaning 'the unjust / the unrighteous' here. The verb آید is present-simple/gnomic in tone (classical usage often renders it as 'comes' or 'will come').
segments.s339-g57: چنانچون is a subordinating conjunction meaning “as / when” introducing the time clause; it precedes the name. The proper name فریدون follows, and فرخ‌نژاد is an epithet in apposition (a descriptive family-name/attribute, “well‑born”) attached to the name.
segments.s339-g58: بر این مهرگان is a locative phrase: بر (on/at) + این (this/that) + مهرگان (the feast Mehregan). تاج بر سر نهاد is the verb phrase: تاج (the crown, direct object) + بر سر (upon the head) + نهاد (past tense of نهادن ‘to put/place’). Persian here follows a verb‑final/verb‑last tendency though the poet places the verb after the object phrase for flow; نهاد is the simple past ‘placed’.
segments.s339-g59: بدان here is the fused locative/ demonstrative ‘به + آن’ = “at/on that”; مهرگان is the festival. گزین followed by او functions as ‘(they) chose him’ or ‘he was chosen’ (گزین as the selecting action or adjectival ‘chosen’), and ز مهر means ‘out of/by/through مهر’ — i.e. ‘by love/favour’ (مهر = love/favour). The clause is elliptical: subject is omitted (generic/political agents).
segments.s339-g60: کز is a fused conjunction (کَه + ز) meaning roughly ‘from which / because of which’; آن = ‘that’; راستی = ‘truth / rightfulness’ or ‘just claim’ here; رفت is past of رفتن ‘to go/leave’ meaning ‘departed / left’; مهر سپهر = ‘the favour/affection of the sky/heavens’ (مهر governing سپهر). Word order shows a poetic inversion: the verb رفت appears before the noun phrase مهر سپهر, which functions as the thing that departed.
segments.s339-g61: بیاراست is a past verb form of آراستن ‘to adorn / to set in order’ meaning ‘he adorned’; ایوان is the direct object (a porch/iwan); کیخسروی is an attributive name in genitive/apposition indicating the owner (‘of Keykhosrow’). The subject is omitted (implied ‘he’).
segments.s339-g62: بپیراست is another past verb form (ب‑ prefix + پیراست) meaning ‘he arranged/adorned’; دیوان او is the object phrase ‘his divan/court/office’ (دیوان here = royal court/office or bureau), and از نوی is an adverbial phrase ‘from new’ = ‘anew / afresh’. The order places the verb up front for poetic rhythm, with the object following.
segments.s339-g63: چنینست is a fixed copular phrase ‘it is thus / such is’; گیتی = ‘the world’; فراز و نشیب is a paired noun phrase ‘rise and fall / highs and lows’. The order is formulaic: comment (چنینست) followed by the explaining noun phrase.
segments.s339-g64: یکی is the indefinite/number ‘one’ (one party); آورد is past of آوردن ‘to bring’ used here in the sense ‘cause / bring about’; دیگری = ‘the other’; را is the definite object marker attaching to دیگری; نهیب is the object meaning ‘rebuke / stern cry’. The clause means ‘one causes a rebuke to the other’, with را marking the affected noun.
segments.s339-g49: همه مهتران is a noun phrase meaning “all the chiefs/elders” (همه = everyone before the noun). خواندند is past tense, 3rd person plural, “they sang/called out”; آفرین is an exclamation/accusative nominal phrase here (‘Bravo’/an acclamation) that is the content of what they called.
segments.s339-g50: به فرمان is an ablative/agentive phrase “by the command / at the order (of)” with به used in the sense “by.” نهادند is the past verb (3rd pl.) meaning “they set/laid (down).” سر برزمین is the object and locative: سر = “head” and برزمین = “on the ground,” so the verb takes the object ‘heads’ which are placed on the ground.
segments.s339-g51: ز گفتار ایشان is a prepositional phrase ‘from/by their speech’ (ز = poetic from/because of). دلش is a possessed noun with the enclitic ش = ‘his heart.’ گشت is the past of گشتن used here as ‘became/was made,’ so the whole clause means ‘by their speech his heart became fresh/renewed.’
segments.s339-g52: ببالید is a past verb meaning ‘he rejoiced/grew proud’ (here referring back to the heart). The coordinating و links the next result clause. بر دیگر اندازه is a prepositional phrase ‘beyond/above the other/previous measure’ (بر = over/upon, دیگر = other, اندازه = measure/degree). گشت again is ‘became,’ so the clause means he rejoiced and became greater than before.
segments.s339-g53: بر آن نامداران places the action toward ‘those famed/illustrious men’ (بر = upon/toward, آن نامداران = those renowned ones). گرفت is a past tense verb ‘he took/received,’ and آفرین is the thing received (‘acclamation’). Word order here is object‑oriented: he obtained acclamation from/upon those men.
segments.s339-g54: که introduces the content/wish clause that follows. آباد بادا is an optative formula: آباد = ‘prosperous’ and بادا is the archaic optative particle ‘may (it/he) be.’ به گردان زمین is a poetic locution ‘throughout the turning/round of the earth’ (به = in/through, گردان = the revolving/round [here poetic for the world/realm], زمین = earth). The whole clause is a wish: ‘May he be prosperous over the earth.’
segments.s339-g55: گزیدش is past tense ‘he chose’ with the enclitic ‑ش (object) ‘him/it’ attached: ‘he chose him/it.’ یکی روز فرخنده‌تر is a noun phrase ‘a certain (one) more auspicious day’ with فرخنده‌تر comparative/augmentative ‘more auspicious.’ The object clitic on گزیدش refers to the person for whom the day was chosen.
segments.s339-g56: که تا is a stacked pair of subordinating conjunctions marking purpose (classical style): both mean ‘so that/in order that.’ برنهد is an archaic 3rd‑person form ‘may he place / (so that) he sets’ (present/subjunctive sense used for purpose). تاج شاهی به سر is the object phrase ‘the crown of kingship on (his) head’ (تاج شاهی = the royal crown/kingship crown; به سر = upon the head).
segments.s339-g17: چنین (an adverb) modifies the verb and means 'thus/so'. داد is the simple past 'gave' and functions here as the main verb 'answered/gave' with پاسخ as its direct object; ورا is the enclitic pronoun 'to him'. پور سام is the nominative apposition 'the son of Sam' placed after the verb for emphasis (typical epic inversion).
segments.s339-g18: که introduces the reported content ('that...'). خسرو is the proper name (Khosrow); ترا is the second‑person object pronoun 'you'; را here is the object/definiteness marker/emphatic element attached after ترا; شاه is the predicate/noun 'king'; بردست is a compound indicating bestowal or 'by hand' (i.e. conferred); نام is 'name' — together meaning 'Khosrow named you king' (past action reported).
segments.s339-g19: پذیرفته‌ام is the perfect (first person singular) 'I have accepted'. پند و اندرز is a coordinated object 'advice and counsel', and او is the possessive/third‑person pronoun 'his' (postposed, a common classical placement), so the whole phrase is the direct object of پذیرفته‌ام.
segments.s339-g20: نیابد is the negative 3rd‑person verbal form (here with a hortatory/optative force: 'may ... not / shall not find'). گذر پای (literally 'the passage of the foot') is a fixed image for 'passing/crossing'. از مرز او 'over/from his border' locates the forbidden crossing; word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect with the verb up front.
segments.s339-g21: تو شاهی omits the copula and predicates 'you are king' (شاهی used as predicate/noun 'kingship'). و is the coordinating 'and'. ما یکسره کهتریم is 'we are wholly/in every respect inferior' (کهتریم is the adjectival predicate 'we are lesser/inferior'; یکسره is an adverb 'completely/altogether').
segments.s339-g22: ز is the archaic/prepositional 'from/by'. رای here means 'counsel/decision' (advice or official decision), و 'and', ز again 'by/for', فرمان 'command', او 'his' — together with نگذریم (negative present/subjunctive first‑person plural) meaning 'we will not pass/exceed' i.e. 'we will not go beyond / disobey' his counsel and command.
segments.s339-g23: This is a simple enumerative phrase: من و رستم و زابلی 'I and Rostam and the Zabulian(s)'. هرکه هست is an indefinite relative 'whoever there is / whoever is present', qualifying the group; هست is the copula 'is'.
segments.s339-g24: ز (from/of) + مهتر تو 'your chief/elder' — note the possessive follows the noun (archaic/classical order). برنگسلانیم is the negative hortative/first‑person plural form meaning 'we will not sever/detach/withdraw' (بر- prefix + گسلانیم), and دست 'hand' is the object: 'we will not take our hand away (from) your chief' — idiomatically, 'we will not abandon or withdraw support from your leader'.
segments.s339-g1: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. لهراسب is the subject (proper name). آگه شد is a two-word verbal phrase meaning 'became aware / learned' (آگه is an adjective/participial element 'aware', شد is the past tense auxiliary 'became'). از کار شاه is a prepositional complement 'of the king's affair' (از + noun) that completes what he learned.
segments.s339-g2: ز (poetic variant of از) marks source/association 'from/of the troops' and introduces the noun لشکر. که begins a relative clause 'that/who'. بودند is the past plural copula 'they were'. با او به راه is a prepositional phrase 'with him on the road' (با = 'with', او = 'him', به راه = 'on the way'); the relative clause modifies لشکر.
segments.s339-g3: نشست is the past tense verb 'sat / took his seat'. The phrase از بر تخت is a literary/poetic locative expression relating to the throne (بر تخت = 'upon the throne', with the preceding از appearing for poetic word-order). با تاج زر is an instrumental/accompaniment phrase 'with a golden crown' (با = 'with', تاج زر = 'gold crown'—tاج modified by زر).
segments.s339-g4: برفتند is a past plural verbal form 'they departed / marched off' (verb before subject here, common in poetry). گردان is the noun 'troop / regiment' and زرین کمر is a postpositive adjective phrase 'golden-girt' (زرین 'golden' modifying کمر 'girdle/belt', together forming 'gold-belted' or 'gold-girt'), describing the گردان.
segments.s339-g5: به آواز is an adverbial phrase 'in a voice / aloud' (به used instrumentally 'by/with' + آواز 'voice'). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said / called out'. ای سران سپاه is a vocative address: ای 'O', سران 'leaders/chiefs' (plural), سپاه 'of the army' — together 'O chiefs of the army!'
segments.s339-g6: شنیده is the past participle or verbal-perfect 'heard' used here as the verbal predicate. همه is the subject 'all/everyone' placed after the predicate (poetic inversion). پند و اندرز شاه is the object 'the king's advice and counsel' (پند and اندرز are synonymous nouns, joined by و, with شاه in a possessive relation).
segments.s339-g7: هرآنکس که is a standard relative/indefinite 'whoever / anyone who'. از تخت من is a prepositional phrase 'about/from my throne' (in poetry this signals relation to the throne). نیست شاد is the negative copula plus predicate in inverted order (نیست = 'is not', شاد = 'happy/glad'); the inversion (predicate after نیست) is characteristic of poetic syntax.
segments.s339-g8: ندارد is the present/past-stem form 'does not have / will not have' used here to mean 'does not hold/retain'. همی is an adverb meaning 'always / ever / indeed' (poetic emphasis). پند شاهان به یاد is a verbal-nominal phrase where پند شاهان is 'the advice of kings' and به یاد ('in memory' / 'in mind') completes the sense 'to have X in mind' — together 'does not keep the kings' counsel in memory / does not remember the kings' advice.'
segments.s339-g33: This is a nominal subject phrase listing a title plus two epithets/names joined by و. جهاندار is a title 'world‑ruler' and نیک‌اختر and شادروز are proper epithets/names; no verb appears in this segment, so it functions as the agent/subject for the following verb.
segments.s339-g34: شما را is the object (شما = 'you', را = definite/object marker) placed before the verb; سپرد is a simple past verb 'entrusted/handed over.' آن زمان نیمروز is a temporal adverbial 'at that time, midday' placed after the verb. The segment therefore says that the previously named agent did the entrusting at noon.
segments.s339-g35: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now' placed frontally. پادشاهی is the noun 'kingship/kingdom.' جز آن هرچه هست is a restrictive phrase 'except that which exists' (جز + آن + هرچه هست), where هرچه هست = 'whatever there is.' The whole segment establishes what portion of the realm is meant.
segments.s339-g36: بگیرید is a present-subjunctive/imperative form (2nd person plural polite) used as an order 'take/seize.' چنان/چندان که introduces a measure clause 'to the extent that...' (چنان/چندان + که ...). باید is the modal 'ought/should,' and به دست here means 'into your hand' in the sense of 'into your power/control' (دست = power). The clause therefore specifies how much they are to take.
segments.s339-g37: مرا (here tokenized as م + را) is the first‑person object 'me' with را marking the object; با شما means 'with you' (accompaniment). گنج بخشیده isn't a simple noun + adjective: بخشیده is the past participle 'given/granted' and with نیست forms a negative perfect/existential 'has not been given.' The clause says 'I have not been granted treasure along with you.'
segments.s339-g38: This is a three‑item nominative list (تن و دوده و پادشاهی) followed by the predicate یکیست 'is one.' یکیست is a copular assertion 'is one' meaning 'are the same' or 'are one and the same,' a typical poetic / philosophical claim that body, dust/smoke and kingship amount to the same (transient) thing.
segments.s339-g39: به گودز گفت is a reporting clause 'he said to Goudarz' with به marking the indirect object. آنچه داری نهان is the content clause: آنچه داری = 'what you have/whatsoever you possess' and نهان can function adverbially 'secretly' or adjectivally 'hidden.' Here it introduces the material of the reported command that follows.
segments.s339-g40: بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive form meaning 'speak/tell' (here used as a command). از دل = 'from the heart' (از + دل). ای پهلوان جهان is a vocative 'O champion of the world' addressing the addressee; note in the line the vocative ای is attached poetically (دل‌ای) but functions as an address. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic force: verb — adverbial — vocative.
segments.s339-g25: هرآنکس که introduces an indefinite relative clause ('whoever, whoever...'). او is the subject 'he' and نه is the negation placed before the following prepositional phrase; بر این ره is a prepositional phrase 'on/along this road/path' (poetic word order omits an ezāfe). بود is the past copula 'was'. Altogether: 'whoever he was not on this path.'
segments.s339-g26: ز نیکی 'from/with respect to goodness' gives the domain (i.e. as regards good deeds). ورا is the enclitic 'to him/for him'. دست کوته بود is a predicate: literally 'the hand was short'—an idiom meaning 'he lacked power/help/generosity'; کوته is an adjective 'short' and بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s339-g27: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. لهراسب is the subject (proper name). گفتار دستان is a noun phrase 'the speech/words of Dastān' (poetic genitive without an overt ezāfe), and شنید is the past verb 'heard'. The clause therefore means 'When Lohrāsb heard Dastān's speech/words.'
segments.s339-g28: بدو is the dative 'to him' (i.e. 'to/at him'); آفرین here is an acclamation/praise given to someone, and کرد is the light verb 'made/did' forming the verbal phrase 'praised him'. و coordinates the next action. دم درکشید is an idiomatic sequence: دم 'breath' + درکشید 'drew in/pulled' — 'he drew in his breath' (i.e. paused or took a breath).
segments.s339-g29: چنین گفت is a set phrase 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت here functions as the verbal expression 'said' in poetic diction). کز ('که ز') introduces the following specification 'from/by the judge of truth' — داور راستی = 'the judge of truth' (an epithet for the divine or just judge).
segments.s339-g30: شما را is the dative/beneficiary marked by را ('for you'). مبادا is a hortatory/prohibitive particle meaning 'may it not be' or 'let there not be'. کم و کاستی is the noun phrase 'lack and deficiency' — the whole is a wish: 'may there not be any lack or deficiency for you.'
segments.s339-g31: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'. یزدان is the subject 'God' (divine being). شما را is the object/dative 'you'. بدان is a fused prepositional phrase (به + آن) meaning 'for that / to that end' (poetic contracted form), and آفرید is the past verb 'created/made' — 'that God created you for that.'
segments.s339-g32: که again introduces a result/purpose clause 'so that'. روی بدیها is the noun phrase 'the face/manifestation of evils' (روی = face/appearance, بدیها = evils). شود is the present/future-like verbal form 'become' (here expressing result or potential), and ناپدید is the predicate adjective 'vanished/unseen' — 'so that the appearance of evils becomes unseen.'
segments.s339-g9: مرا (م + را) is the accusative/indirect-object marker meaning “me” (object of فرمود/گفت). هرچه is a free relative “whatever.” فرمود و گفت are two verbs with the same (omitted) third-person subject — here ‘he commanded and said.’ آن is a resumptive pronoun referring back to هرچه, and کنم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive used here to express the speaker’s intention (“I will do”). Word order places the object (مرا) and relative (هرچه) before the verbs, and the clause closes with the 1st-person verb کنم.
segments.s339-g10: بکوشم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive of کوشیدن with the prefix ب- (classical/polite form) meaning “I will strive” or “I shall try.” به نیکی is a prepositional phrase “toward/for goodness” meaning “to do good” or “in kindness.” و links an additional purpose phrase, فرمان کنم: فرمان is the object “the order/command,” and کنَم is again 1st-person subjunctive “I do/perform,” so the clause states the speaker’s resolve to strive to do good and to obey or carry out the command.
segments.s339-g11: شما نیز = “you too/also.” از اندرز او is a prepositional phrase “from his counsel/advice” (اندرز = counsel; او = his). دست باز is an idiomatic nominal phrase (دست + باز) meaning to “let go/withdraw (one’s hand)” or “cease one’s support”; the full negative or prohibitive force comes when مدارید follows in the next segment. Word order places the addressed person and the prepositional phrase before the verbal idea expressed by مدارید.
segments.s339-g12: مدارید here functions as a 2nd-person-plural jussive/imperative form meaning roughly “do not (withdraw)/do not cease” in the poetic register. وز is the fused form of و + از (“and from / and do not from”), so وز من = “and from me.” مدارید repeats as the verb governing راز, i.e. “do not withhold/keep secret.” راز is the object “secret.” The construction therefore issues two prohibitions: don’t withdraw from his advice, and don’t conceal the secret from me.
segments.s339-g13: گنهکار is a predicate adjective/noun “sinner” or “sinful.” باشد is the 3rd-person subjunctive/present of بودن used here to state existence or judgment (“is”/“may be”). به یزدان is a prepositional phrase meaning “before God” or “in God’s sight.” کسی is the indefinite subject “one” or “anyone,” which is then specified by the following relative clause introduced by که.
segments.s339-g14: که is the relative pronoun “who/that.” اندرز شاهان is a genitive phrase “the counsel/advice of the kings” (شاهان = kings). ندارد is the 3rd-person singular present of داشتن meaning “does not have” or more idiomatically here “gives no heed to / lacks.” بسی is an adverbial intensifier (classical) meaning “much” or “at all,” used here to emphasize the lack: “has little/no (regard).” The verb نـدارد comes at the end, as typical in Persian clause order.
segments.s339-g15: بد و نیک = “bad and good” (enumerating both kinds of things). از این = “about this / concerning this.” هرچه دارید is a free-relative phrase “whatever you have/possess” with دارید (2nd-person plural present of داشتن) meaning here “you recall/have in mind.” یاد is the noun “remembrance/memory” used with this word order to mean “what you remember” — together: “whatever good or bad about this you have in memory.”
segments.s339-g16: سراسر is an adverb meaning “throughout / entirely / in full.” به من = “to me” (indirect object). بر بباید گشاد is a classical arrangement: بباید is the modal “must/ought,” گشاد (past-participial/adjectival) “open” or “revealed,” and the preverbal particle بر gives a sense of exposure/revelation (so that the whole phrase means “must be opened/revealed to me”). Together: “it must be made fully open to me” / “tell it to me in full and openly.”
segments.s339-g41: بدو گفت گودرز من یک تنم is ordered as a short reported clause: بدو (to him) + گفت (said) + گودرز (Goudarz) — an inversion common in epic Persian where the indirect object / prepositional pronoun appears before گفت. من یک تنم is a nominal predicate with the copula omitted: من (I) + یک تن (one person/one man) + -م as the 1st‑person suffix attached to the predicate (تنم) serving the function of 'I am one person.'
segments.s339-g42: چو introduces a comparison/likeness here ('as' / 'like'). The list that follows names heroes joined by و (and). The proper names are conjoined with و; the exact reading of بی‌گیو is uncertain in the manuscript/lemma given, so its identification is left open (hence sense -1). The final item is بیژنم (Bijan) with the clitic/ending preserved as part of the name.
segments.s339-g43: بر آنم سراسر that introduces the speaker's settled opinion: بر آنم (I am inclined / I am of that mind) with سراسر as an adverb 'entirely' modifying the state. که begins the subordinate clause 'that …' and دستان بگفت is an impersonal/idiomatic phrase 'the tales/what is told say' (دستان = tales/deeds), with بگفت as the archaic past third‑person 'said'/'tell'.
segments.s339-g44: جز این من ندارم سخن در نهفت is a negative existential statement framed classically: جز این (except this) + من ندارم (I do not have) + سخن در نهفت (speech/words hidden within). در نهفت is a compact attributive phrase meaning 'hidden inside' (در = in, نهفت = hidden/kept). The result: 'I have no hidden words except this.'
segments.s339-g45: چنانم که ... is the formula 'I am such that …' (چنانم = 'I am thus/so'), که introduces the content clause. با شاه is the prepositional object 'to/with the king' (here 'to the king'), گفتم is the 1st‑person past ('I said' / 'I spoke') and نخست is an adverb 'first' — so: 'I told the king first.' Note: the lemma senses given for گفتم do not list the simple verb 'to say' and are therefore marked -1.
segments.s339-g46: بدین مایه نشکست عهد درست is a short result/explanatory clause: بدین (by this / with this means — the form is the preposition + demonstrative contracted in epic diction), مایه = 'cause/means', نشکست = 'did not break' (ن + شکست, negative past), and عهد درست = 'the true/firm covenant.' The clause says that by this means the covenant was not broken.
segments.s339-g47: تو شاهی is a nominal sentence 'you are king' with no copula expressed (typical in Persian). و coordinates the second clause: ما سر به سر کهتریم — سر به سر is an idiomatic intensifier 'all / completely / one and all', and کهتریم is the 1st‑person‑plural predicate 'we are inferior' (comparative/adjectival). Together: 'You are king and we are all inferior.'
segments.s339-g48: ز پیمان و فرمان تو نگذریم is a declarative commitment: ز (from/of) introduces the objects پیمان و فرمان (the covenant and the command) + تو (your). نگذریم is a negative present/subjunctive form here used as a resolute pledge 'we will not transgress/abandon' (lit. 'we do not pass/let pass'). The whole phrase: 'we will not break/abandon your covenant and command.'

sentences.s339-s33: Because of this affair we left the king's presence and returned to Lohrasp's affairs.
sentences.s339-s34: May the lofty king be victorious; in him lie both hope for good and the threat of harm.
sentences.s339-s35: He brought goodness to the hearts of friends; harm comes from him upon the unjust.
sentences.s339-s29: When Fereydun, the well-born, placed the crown upon his head at that Mehregan.
sentences.s339-s30: At that Mehregan he was chosen out of love; from that rightfulness the favour of the heavens departed.
sentences.s339-s31: He adorned the iwan of Keykhosrow; he arranged his divan anew.
sentences.s339-s32: Such is the world — heights and lows; one brings (about) a rebuke to the other.
sentences.s339-s25: All the chiefs cried “Bravo”; at the command they laid their heads on the ground.
sentences.s339-s26: Their words renewed his heart; he exulted and became greater than before.
sentences.s339-s27: He received acclamation from those illustrious men: “May he be prosperous throughout the world.”
sentences.s339-s28: He chose for him a more auspicious day so that he might set the crown of kingship upon his head.
sentences.s339-s9: Thus the son of Sam answered him that Khosrow had named you king.
sentences.s339-s10: I have accepted his advice and counsel; may my foot not pass beyond his border.
sentences.s339-s11: You are king, and we are entirely inferior; we will not go against his counsel or his command.
sentences.s339-s12: Rostam and I and every Zabulian who is here — we will not withdraw our hand from your chief.
sentences.s339-s1: When Lohrasp learned of the king's affair from the troops who were on the road with him.
sentences.s339-s2: He took his seat on the throne wearing a golden crown; the golden-girt regiments marched off.
sentences.s339-s3: He called out in a loud voice, 'O chiefs of the army!' — everyone heard the king's advice and counsel.
sentences.s339-s4: Whoever is not glad about my throne will not keep the kings' counsel in mind.
sentences.s339-s17: The world-ruler, Nīk-akhtar and Shādruz, entrusted you at that noon.
sentences.s339-s18: Now take the kingdom except that one — whatever there is — as much as ought to be in your hand.
sentences.s339-s19: He has not granted me treasure together with you; body, soot/smoke and kingship are one.
sentences.s339-s20: He said to Goudarz: 'Tell (secretly) whatever you have — speak from the heart, O champion of the world.'
sentences.s339-s13: Whoever was not on this path, his hand was short of goodness.
sentences.s339-s14: When Lohrāsb heard Dastān's words, he praised him and drew in his breath.
sentences.s339-s15: He said thus: by the judge of truth, may there be no lack or deficiency for you.
sentences.s339-s16: that God created you for that purpose, so that the face of evils may become unseen.
sentences.s339-s5: Whatever he commanded and said to me, I will do; I will strive to act kindly and to carry out the command.
sentences.s339-s6: You also, do not withdraw your hand from his counsel, and do not hide the secret from me.
sentences.s339-s7: He is a sinner in the sight of God who pays no heed to the counsel of kings.
sentences.s339-s8: Whatever good or bad you remember about this, you must tell me fully and openly.
sentences.s339-s21: Goudarz said to him, “I am a single man, as (are) Bī‑Giv and Roham and Bijan.”
sentences.s339-s22: I am wholly of that mind, as the tales say; I have no words hidden except this.
sentences.s339-s23: I am such that I told the king first; by this means the true covenant did not break.
sentences.s339-s24: You are king, and we are altogether inferior; we will not abandon your covenant and command.
